briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for June 3, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Climate Crisis: Global Impacts Materializing
sections.content: Today's reported news once again reminds us of the widespread and severe global impacts of climate change. According to new research, climate change could shift hailstorms towards the Earth's poles and increase the risk of crop damage from hail [9, 11], while South Korean beekeepers are already calculating the costs of damage due to climate change [10, 21]. Furthermore, climate change poses a direct threat to our daily lives and economic activities, including increasing the risk of slower play and reduced player performance at the 2026 World Cup and even altering the nutritional content of tangerines [15, 23]. Notably, climate change has emerged as the greatest threat to global food security [32], and has been identified as a women's health crisis [27], even sparking discussions on whether it should be declared a global health emergency [41]. In Europe, despite scorching heat arriving before the meteorological summer began and breaking May temperature records, concerns are being raised about the continued lack of preparedness for extreme heat [45].

sections.title: ⚠️ El Niño and Climate Change: Accelerating Interactions
sections.content: Concerns are growing recently regarding the interaction between climate change and the El Niño phenomenon. Gareth Redmond-King has warned that El Niño is accelerating climate change [39], and questions have been raised about the possibility of this year's El Niño being stronger due to climate change [26]. The National Centre for Atmospheric Science has explained the impact of El Niño on global weather and the UK, and its relationship with climate change [42]. Thus, the impacts of El Niño are receiving worldwide attention, to the extent that they are covered in major climate-related news on June 3, 2026 [47], and it is a time to closely monitor its ripple effects.

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Climate Action and Renewable Energy Transition Efforts
sections.content: Amidst the global climate crisis, domestic and international cooperation and practical efforts to address climate change continue. South Korea and China have collaborated, holding a clean energy workshop to address the climate crisis [2], and Chungju City has successively organized ecological preservation and carbon neutrality initiatives in commemoration of Environment Day [3]. Domestically, the Export-Import Bank is supporting Samsung Electronics' partners in their transition to renewable energy, strengthening supply chain ESG responses [4, 7], and interest and investment in renewable energy expansion are accelerating, with investments flowing into solar ETFs in response to international oil price fluctuations [5, 6, 8]. Internationally, Australia has proposed providing $500 million to support climate change response in the Pacific region [43], and creative approaches are also being explored, such as the discussion in London about using 'beavers' as an effective and affordable solution to flood issues caused by climate change [38].

sections.title: 🌍 Challenges in Policy and Monitoring Systems
sections.content: While the importance of political will and scientific monitoring systems in addressing climate change is emphasized, several challenges have been reported. Criticism has been raised that 'national climate crisis adaptation measures' should not stop at the border [1], and concerns have emerged in some regions that politics will exacerbate the impacts of climate change [13]. Furthermore, alongside criticism that the costs of climate change are being borne by everyone except the polluters [14], policy and legal difficulties persist, such as the dismissal of lawsuits by young Americans challenging the Trump administration's approach to climate change [25, 37, 40]. Moreover, the Earth is losing key ocean sensors essential for tracking climate change [36], and the Trump administration's announcement of plans to dismantle a $368 million ocean observation system that has provided crucial data for over a decade has caused disappointment [44]. In this context, the analysis that China's CO2 emissions increased by 2% in the first quarter of 2026 [46] once again highlights the urgency of carbon reduction efforts.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has clearly illustrated the severity of the climate crisis, the diverse efforts being made to overcome it, and the challenges that still need to be addressed. This is a time that calls for sustained attention and action. This concludes today's climate briefing.

summary.phenomenon: The Trump administration plans to dismantle the Ocean Observatories Initiative (OOI), a $368 million deep-sea observation system that has provided crucial data on ocean systems and climate change for over a decade. This system is a vast ocean observation network comprising more than 900 instruments.
summary.cause: Due to budget cuts by the Trump administration, the National Science Foundation (NSF) announced it had initiated the 'descoping' of the OOI.
